coaching.coaching: Plan how git diff command completeness for negative proof will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: git diff command completeness for negative proof

coaching.coaching: Realign finding_contract AC alignment with parent requirements to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.
coaching.subject: finding_contract AC alignment with parent requirements

coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria specificity and measurability gaps with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria specificity and measurability gaps

coaching.coaching: Pin down gold set mandatory input field requirement with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: gold set mandatory input field requirement

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on live eval run failure and recovery policy: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: live eval run failure and recovery policy

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for calibration_metrics function selection rationale.
coaching.subject: calibration_metrics function selection rationale

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for expected_counterexample schema as reusable contract — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: expected_counterexample schema as reusable contract

coaching.coaching: Realign agreement threshold alignment with parent gate spec to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the path-restricted git diff in AC 9 with `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD` piped to a check that the only changed file under `.rebar/prompts/` or `src/rebar/llm/` matching calibration intent is `plan-review-project-portability.md`, e.g. `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD | grep -E '^(\.rebar/prompts/|src/rebar/llm/)' | grep -v 'plan-review-project-portability\.md'` asserts empty output.
evidence: Proving command: `git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD -- .rebar/prompts/plan-review-project-portability.md .rebar/criteria_routing.json src/rebar/llm`

By supplying explicit path arguments, git diff only reports on those paths — it does not show diffs to files outside those paths, making the command incapable of proving 'the only calibration edit is the project prompt'.
Defect taxonomy type (4): fixture invalidity — the command does not exercise the claim being made.
findings.finding: AC 9's git diff proving command restricts the diff to a specific set of paths, so it cannot prove the negative claim that no OTHER file outside those paths was modified by calibration; the command returns an empty or single-file result even when unrelated files were also changed, silently passing the check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 9: 'Any calibration-driven edit touches only...'
scenarios: An executor edits both the prompt and a registry file; git diff filtered to those paths shows both files, but a script checking for 'only one file changed' would need to count lines; worse, edits to unscoped files are invisible to this command.
Automated CI gating on this command's output would give a false green if any out-of-scope file was also modified.
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
# Passes only when no calibration-scoped file outside the prompt was touched
git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD \
  | grep -E '^(\.rebar/prompts/|src/rebar/llm/|\.rebar/criteria_routing\.json)' \
  | grep -v 'plan-review-project-portability\.md' \
  && echo 'FAIL: unexpected file touched' && exit 1 || echo 'PASS'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to the leaf that creates and proves a '-k finding_contract' test (as required by the parent), or explicitly reconcile the difference between 'counterexample_metadata' and 'finding_contract' with the parent so both contracts are satisfied.
evidence: Parent AC: 'indigoid-unmystic-serpent proves every must-fire case yields the four-part finding contract: plan citation, alternate client shape, causal mechanism, and observable breakage. Verify: .venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py -k finding_contract'

Leaf has no AC referencing '-k finding_contract'; the leaf's AC for the four-part contract uses '-k counterexample_metadata', proving only that the four metadata fields are non-empty, not that the emitted finding itself carries all four elements
findings.finding: The parent's AC for indigoid-unmystic-serpent explicitly requires a test key '-k finding_contract' ('proves every must-fire case yields the four-part finding contract … Verify: .venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py -k finding_contract'). The leaf never specifies or creates a test named 'finding_contract'; its closest AC uses '-k counterexample_metadata', which checks that four metadata fields are non-empty but does not prove the finding itself carries the four-part contract (citation, alternate shape, causal mechanism, observable breakage). The parent AC is unrepresented in the leaf.
findings.location: Leaf Acceptance Criteria — 'counterexample_metadata' AC; Parent AC 'indigoid-unmystic-serpent proves every must-fire case yields the four-part finding contract'
scenarios: The parent's completion gate checks '.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py -k finding_contract'; that test never exists because the leaf only creates 'counterexample_metadata'. The parent AC fails at completion time.
A reviewer checking the parent's AC cannot find 'finding_contract' among the leaf's deliverables, creating an audit gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a leaf AC: '[ ] Every must-fire case's inline `input` plan includes language that would produce a finding carrying all four parts of the contract (plan citation, alternate client shape, causal mechanism, observable breakage). Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py -k finding_contract`.' Then implement the corresponding test.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the gold_set description in the Approach section to explicitly require each of the eight entries to carry an `input` text field alongside the `finding`/`pass` `label`, matching the contract enforced by `validate_dataset_and_gold`.
evidence: `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py:269-271`: `if not isinstance(g, dict) or not g.get('input') or not g.get('label'): errs.append(f'strict: gold_set[{i}] needs an `input` and a `label`')`
All existing specs (plan-review-g7, plan-review-isf-finder, plan-review-finder) confirm gold entries need `input` + `label`.
The plan says 'labels all eight dataset cases' without specifying each entry's required `input` field.
findings.finding: The plan's gold_set description omits the mandatory `input` field each gold entry must carry. `validate_dataset_and_gold` (the CI strict-mode validator) rejects any gold entry missing `input` or `label`, but the plan only specifies that entries carry 'matching `finding` or `pass` labels' — leaving the `input` key unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The `gold_set` contains all eight dataset cases with matching `finding` or `pass` labels.'
scenarios: Author writes gold_set entries with only case IDs and labels (e.g. `{id: PORT-F1, label: finding}`), which fails `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` because `input` is missing.
AC-6 (`gold_set` structural test using `validate_eval_spec`) fails at CI because the gold entries lack `input`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'Each gold_set entry carries an `input` field (the plain-text scenario description, matching or summarizing the corresponding dataset case) plus a `label` of `finding` or `pass`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief failure-handling policy to the Approach or Test Strategy section: specify whether a failed epoch must be discarded and the full N-run set restarted, or whether at_least(2) successful epochs satisfy the gate, so the operator-attested AC is unambiguous.
evidence: 'Run each dataset case three times through rebar criteria eval' — no retry or failure-handling policy stated
'epochs: 3' / 'gate: at_least(2)' — statistical gate, not error-recovery semantics
Operator attestation AC requires recording 'run count' and 'aggregate metrics' but does not define what constitutes an acceptable partial result
No mention of timeout, backoff, or re-run guidance if the Anthropic API is unavailable mid-epoch
findings.finding: The plan specifies three live LLM eval runs against Anthropic Claude but provides no guidance on what happens if one or more runs fail mid-execution (API timeout, rate limit, network error). The `gate: at_least(2)` and `epochs: 3` fields are quality thresholds for valid results, not error-recovery semantics. If a run is abandoned mid-stream, it is unclear whether the operator must restart all three runs, whether partial epoch results are discarded, or whether the operator-attested AC can be satisfied with only two completed runs.
findings.location: Approach section — 'run each dataset case three times through rebar criteria eval'; AC: '[operator-attested] Three live runs meet recall 1.0…'
scenarios: Claude API returns a 529 rate-limit error after epoch 2 of 3; operator does not know whether to count the two completed epochs or restart entirely
Network flap causes epoch 3 to time out; partial results are ambiguous against the recall/kappa thresholds
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'If any epoch fails (timeout, API error, or partial response), discard that epoch and rerun it before computing aggregate metrics; the operator-attested AC requires exactly three *completed* epochs recorded in the ticket comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Approach section explaining why `calibrate_criterion` (not `run_eval`/`rebar prompt eval`) is the chosen live-run path — specifically that it is the only path producing per-case stability and Cohen's kappa, which are both required by the plan's release thresholds.
evidence: Plan states: 'Use the existing project eval override path proven by src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md' — but does not explain why calibrate_criterion (not run_eval) is the execution path.
calibrate_criterion in eval.py returns kappa, stability_min, and per-case stability which run_eval does NOT produce (run_eval returns epoch_pass, JUnit, passed).
The release threshold requires 'Cohen kappa >= 0.70' and 'per-case stability >= 0.6666...' — only the calibrate_criterion path computes these.
ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan explains the calibrate_criterion vs. run_eval distinction or why the former is needed.
findings.finding: The plan states a positive rationale for the override-path mechanism ('Use the existing project eval override path proven by README.plan-review.md') but gives no positive rationale for choosing `calibrate_criterion` (the `rebar criteria eval` CLI path) over the `run_eval` / `rebar prompt eval` path. Since `calibrate_criterion` is the only path that produces the per-case stability and Cohen's kappa the plan's release thresholds require, the absence of this explanation leaves the rationale incomplete — a reader unfamiliar with the distinction between the two paths cannot confirm the approach is correct without reading the source.
findings.location: Approach section / AC 8 (`rebar criteria eval project.portability --runs 3 --output json`)
scenarios: An implementer unfamiliar with the two paths might implement structural tests against `run_eval` output (JUnit/epoch_pass) instead of `calibrate_criterion` output (kappa/stability), causing the injected-perfect-solve test (AC 7) to assert non-existent keys.
A reviewer of the plan cannot confirm the mechanism is correct without cross-referencing eval.py source.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, after 'run the existing live evaluation runner', add: 'The `rebar criteria eval` command invokes `calibrate_criterion` (not `run_eval`) because it is the only path that produces per-case stability fractions and Cohen's kappa — both required by the release threshold; `run_eval` produces JUnit/epoch-gate output and does not compute these metrics.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (or AC) to update `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` with the `expected_counterexample` metadata schema — field names, types, and the rule that all four must be non-empty for every `expect: finding` case.
evidence: Plan defines four required metadata fields for must-fire cases but contains no instruction to update README.plan-review.md or any design doc with this schema.
The plan references `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` as the authoritative reference for the eval override path, making it the natural home for this extension.
ABSENCE: no 'update README' or 'add ADR' step appears anywhere in Approach, Acceptance Criteria, or Test Strategy.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new corpus metadata schema — four required `expected_counterexample` fields (`plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, `observable_breakage`) — as a reusable contract for must-fire eval cases, but does not call for updating `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` or any other doc to capture this schema. Future corpus authors have no canonical reference for what these fields mean or that they are required.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Each must-fire case uses `expect: finding`, includes an inline `input` plan, and records `expected_counterexample` metadata with non-empty `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, and `observable_breakage` fields'
scenarios: A developer authors a second criterion-specific eval corpus and omits `expected_counterexample` fields because no doc describes them as required.
A reviewer inspects the YAML and cannot determine whether the metadata fields are optional annotations or load-bearing schema enforced by the scorer.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one bullet to the Approach or Acceptance Criteria: 'Update `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` to document the `expected_counterexample` object schema (`plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, `observable_breakage`), noting that all four fields are required and must be non-empty for every `expect: finding` dataset entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `expected_counterexample` four-field schema is intended to become a reusable convention (if so, add it to `validate_dataset_and_gold`'s `metadata_keys` or a new validation path and document it in `README.plan-review.md`); if it is only needed as inline documentation for this one corpus, simplify it to free-form YAML comments or a single `note:` field that already exists in `metadata_keys`.
evidence: Zero hits for `expected_counterexample`, `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, or `observable_breakage` in the entire codebase outside the plan-review cache.

`validate_dataset_and_gold` in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py` lists a fixed `metadata_keys` set (`id`, `corpus`, `expect`, `criterion`, `kind`, `pair`, `mode`, `note`, `label`) and does not include these four fields — they are inert YAML that no existing machinery enforces.
Only 4 call-sites in one eval file (PORT-F1..F4). Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 existing call-sites before a schema abstraction is non-premature; these 4 are all in the same new file.
The plan acknowledges `test_criteria_eval.py` as prior art — that file's must-fire cases use only `id`, `expect`, `input` with no `expected_counterexample` sub-schema.
findings.finding: The `expected_counterexample` dict (four required non-empty fields: `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, `observable_breakage`) is a new case-metadata schema with only 4 use-sites (PORT-F1..PORT-F4 in this one eval file). It is not read by any existing scorer, not validated by `validate_dataset_and_gold` or `validate_eval_spec`, and is not documented as a reusable convention — yet the plan treats it as a formal contract enforced by a dedicated AC (`counterexample_metadata`). Rule-of-Three: a 4-site schema introduced as a standard before any second corpus exists is premature abstraction.
findings.location: Approach section — 'records `expected_counterexample` metadata with non-empty `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, and `observable_breakage` fields'; AC `counterexample_metadata`
scenarios: The schema is enforced only by the new test; if a second portability eval corpus is authored later without these fields, the counterexample_metadata AC passes vacuously (the test only covers this one spec).
Future corpus authors see the schema in one YAML file with no README entry and no validator backing, and cannot determine whether it is required, optional, or advisory.
findings.suggested_fix: If the schema is corpus-documentation only (no scorer reads it), replace the four-field sub-dict with the existing `note:` metadata key (already in `metadata_keys`) and move the structured content to a YAML comment. If it is intended to be a reusable contract, add validation in `validate_dataset_and_gold` and document it in `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/README.plan-review.md` before shipping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'agreement: 1.0' from the leaf's release threshold and operator-attested AC (or first add it to the parent's operator-attested AC so the leaf is again a consistent narrowing), since the parent does not specify an 'agreement' gate.
evidence: Parent operator-attested AC: 'indigoid-unmystic-serpent records three live eval runs with recall 1.0, false-accept rate 0.0, per-case stability of at least two of three, and Cohen kappa of at least 0.70' — no 'agreement' metric
Leaf Approach: 'The release threshold is recall 1.0, false-accept rate 0.0, agreement: 1.0, per-case stability >= 0.6666666667, and Cohen kappa >= 0.70'
Leaf operator-attested AC: 'Three live runs meet recall 1.0, false-accept 0.0, agreement 1.0, per-case stability of at least 0.6666666667, and Cohen kappa of at least 0.70'
findings.finding: The leaf introduces 'agreement: 1.0' as a named release-gate threshold in both its Approach section and its operator-attested AC, but the parent's operator-attested AC specifies only four thresholds: recall 1.0, false-accept rate 0.0, per-case stability of at least two of three, and Cohen kappa of at least 0.70. No 'agreement' metric appears anywhere in the parent's plan. The leaf is adding a new release gate the parent does not contain.
findings.location: Approach section ('The release threshold is recall 1.0, false-accept rate 0.0, agreement: 1.0, per-case stability >= 0.6666666667, and Cohen kappa >= 0.70') and operator-attested AC ('Three live runs meet recall 1.0, false-accept 0.0, agreement 1.0, per-case stability of at least 0.6666666667, and Cohen kappa of at least 0.70')
scenarios: The live eval run passes the parent's four thresholds but agreement falls below 1.0 — the leaf's operator-attested AC fails even though the parent's gate would have been satisfied, silently blocking delivery.
The parent epic's completion gate checks only its own ACs; the leaf and parent give different answers about whether the live run constitutes a pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) delete 'agreement: 1.0' from the Approach release threshold and the operator-attested AC so the leaf's gate exactly matches the parent's four-threshold contract, or (b) update the parent's operator-attested AC to add 'agreement: 1.0' first, then re-review the leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the `expected_counterexample` sub-schema is enforced by the shared eval infrastructure (e.g., extend `validate_dataset_and_gold`'s metadata-key check or add a README entry to `eval_specs/`) so the processing rule is co-located with the spec-validation machinery, not siloed in a single bespoke test.
evidence: Zero hits for `expected_counterexample`, `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, or `observable_breakage` in src/rebar/llm/evals/ or src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/.

`validate_dataset_and_gold` in eval.py:223 defines `metadata_keys = {id, corpus, expect, criterion, kind, pair, mode, note, label}` — the four `expected_counterexample` fields are absent, so a case lacking them passes strict validation.
The existing prior-art test cases in `test_criteria_eval.py` use only `id`, `expect`, `input` — the `expected_counterexample` sub-schema is entirely novel to this plan.

The plan co-locates the schema definition with the YAML case specification but provides no decision rule (a validation hook, a scorer, or a README entry) that makes the contract machine-enforced beyond the single bespoke AC test.
findings.finding: The `expected_counterexample` metadata schema (four required fields: `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, `observable_breakage`) is introduced as a formal case-level contract, but no processing rule co-located with the schema validator enforces it — `validate_dataset_and_gold` does not list these keys in `metadata_keys` or check them, and `validate_eval_spec` is silent about them. The new spec will load and pass strict validation even with all four fields absent or empty; the plan's AC `counterexample_metadata` enforces these fields only via a bespoke test with no runtime backstop.
findings.location: Approach section — 'records `expected_counterexample` metadata with non-empty `plan_citation`, `alternate_shape`, `causal_mechanism`, and `observable_breakage` fields'; AC `counterexample_metadata`
scenarios: An executor authors the YAML with missing `expected_counterexample` fields; `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` and `load_eval_spec` both succeed silently, making the `counterexample_metadata` AC test the only guard.
A future corpus author copies the eval file pattern without the sub-schema; nothing in the shared infrastructure flags the omission.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) promote `test_project_portability_criterion.py` into this plan's deliverables with an explicit authoring step, or (b) add a hard dependency edge stating this AC can only be verified after the criterion-integration child is merged, and note that in AC 10's text.
evidence: Proving command: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`
Scope section lists only `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py` as in-scope — `test_project_portability_criterion.py` is absent.

Dependencies section says 'Depends on the criterion-integration child' but does not establish that child as a prerequisite gate before this AC is evaluated; defect taxonomy type (9): references a sibling/child ticket's files with no verified dependency edge.
findings.finding: AC 10's proving command references `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py`, a file that is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE for this plan and presumably lives in the named 'criterion-integration child' ticket; if that child is not yet merged, the pytest invocation will error, making the criterion unverifiable within this plan's own scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 10: 'The final project rubric retains every structural marker owned by immune-floury-toad'
scenarios: An executor running the AC 10 proving command before the criterion-integration child is merged receives a collection error from pytest, not a meaningful test result.
CI automation running this plan's full AC suite in isolation will fail AC 10 regardless of this plan's correctness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` to the Scope (or in-scope dependency list) and describe what it must contain, OR replace the final AC's verify command with a proof that does not rely on an out-of-scope file.
evidence: Scope lists exactly: `.rebar/evals/plan-review-project-portability.eval.yaml`, `.rebar/prompts/plan-review-project-portability.md`, `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`
Final AC: 'Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`'
`test_project_portability_criterion.py` is absent from the Scope section and undescribed in Approach or Test Strategy
findings.finding: The final AC's verify command references `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py`, which is not listed in the Scope section and not described anywhere in the plan body — this file is implicitly expected to exist (from a sibling task) but this plan neither creates it nor declares it as a precondition, creating a contradiction between Scope and the verify step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet; Scope section
scenarios: Implementer runs the final AC proof command and `test_project_portability_criterion.py` does not exist because this plan never creates it, causing the gate to fail.
Reviewer cannot confirm the final AC is satisfiable from this plan alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` to the Scope with a brief description of its purpose and note it is delivered by the criterion-integration child (making it an explicit precondition), or (b) split the final AC into two bullets — one verified by `test_project_portability_eval.py` alone (in scope) and one explicitly labeled as gated on the criterion-integration child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `immune-floury-toad` in the plan (e.g. in Approach or a Constraints subsection): specify what entity it identifies, which structural markers it owns, and where those markers appear in the rubric file.
evidence: Final AC: 'The final project rubric retains every structural marker owned by `immune-floury-toad`.'
The string 'immune-floury-toad' appears only in this single AC; no definition, context, or cross-reference exists in Why, What, Scope, Approach, Dependencies, or Test Strategy.
findings.finding: The entity `immune-floury-toad` referenced in the final AC ('retains every structural marker owned by `immune-floury-toad`') is never defined, described, or explained anywhere in the plan body — an implementer cannot know what markers to preserve or how to verify ownership.
scenarios: Implementer edits the prompt file during calibration and has no way to know which structural markers must be preserved.
Reviewer cannot audit compliance because the criterion references an unexplained identifier.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Approach section such as: '`immune-floury-toad` is the owner token embedded in `.rebar/prompts/plan-review-project-portability.md` (e.g., as a comment or metadata field); calibration edits must not remove the lines/blocks tagged with this token.' Then ensure the test verifies that those specific lines are present after any edit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` to this plan's Scope and describe what it must verify, or remove it from the AC-10 prove command and replace with only `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`.
evidence: Grep of `tests/unit/` directory listing: `test_project_portability_criterion.py` is absent.
Search for 'test_project_portability_criterion' across all non-cache files: zero matches.
Plan Scope section lists only `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py` as an in-scope test file.
The prove command runs both files jointly, so if the criterion test file is missing the AC can never be proven.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` is referenced in the final AC's prove command but does not exist in the repository and is not listed in the plan's Scope section.
scenarios: Implementer runs the final AC prove command and gets a pytest 'no such file' collection error, blocking sign-off.
The file belongs to a sibling 'criterion-integration' ticket whose scope was not merged here, leaving a dangling prove command.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC-10 prove command to: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py` (dropping the criterion test file that is out of scope for this calibration ticket).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the reference to `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` in the final AC: either add this file to Scope with a description of what it tests (e.g., rubric structural-marker checks for `immune-floury-toad`), or replace the prove command with one that only references `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`.
evidence: Plan AC text: 'Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`'
Scope section lists only `tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py` as a new test file
Search for `test_project_portability_criterion` across the repo returns zero matches

findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` is referenced in the final AC prove command but is neither listed in Scope, described anywhere in the plan body, nor present in the repository or any dependency ticket's file_impact — it is a hallucinated edit target.
scenarios: The prove command for the final AC will fail with a 'file not found' or 'no tests collected' error since the referenced test file does not exist and the plan never instructs it to be created.
An implementer following the scope section will create `test_project_portability_eval.py` but not `test_project_portability_criterion.py`, causing the AC prove command to fail at review time.

Otherwise, replace the final AC prove command with: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_eval.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py` to the Scope section with a description of what 'structural markers owned by `immune-floury-toad`' means and what the test must verify, or remove this file from the final AC's prove command if the criterion-rubric structural tests are handled by an existing test (e.g., `test_reviewer_prompt_hygiene.py`).
